text: Visible scarring from previous surgeries is observed at the five o'clock position. The recurrent fistula is noted with an internal opening, and a potential external opening at the top of the wound. Radiological images show a defect below the fistula level in both internal and external sphincters.

The surgeon identifies the internal opening of the fistula and follows the track laterally into the buttock. The anorectal junction tissue is visible above the opening. Using a fine lacrimal probe, the surgeon gently inserts it into the internal opening, confirming the absence of muscle below due to previous divisions. The surgeon prepares to cut down onto the probe using a diaphane, ensuring no muscle is cut.

The surgeon uses Emmet forceps to access an intersphincteric cavity identified on MRI, carefully inserting the forceps into the cavity entrance. The cavity measures approximately two to three millimeters in the intersphincteric space, and drainage is provided without involving muscle tissue.

The video shows a complex anal fistula with a large external opening exhibiting significant overgranulation. The fistula track is long and deep, running from the front to the right side of the anus, then turning towards the back and connecting to an internal opening. An additional extension is visible at the corner of the track, and the tract itself is notably wide, especially at its midpoint.

The surgeon uses a right-angle probe to identify the internal opening of the fistula, directing it from eight o'clock to nine o'clock as it navigates through the long track. The VAFT scope, equipped with a camera, light leads, and instrument port, is introduced into the main track. The surgeon attempts to maneuver the probe through the complex track, encountering a cul-de-sac, and plans to place a seton at the corner to facilitate a sphincter-preserving procedure.
